title: Eva vs Gundam as commentaries on war audiences
display_title: Eva vs Gundam as commentaries on war audiences
wikitext:
[]: Re: reanimated by scrub coral to sing "Hello my baby&qu             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jul 29, 2015 3:37 am Alaska Slim wrote: But Gundam already did that. Evangelion went a step further, to completely denied otaku an idol they would want to relate to, and instead, gave them someone who better embodied the qualities they shared, and realistically depicted the result.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Wed Jul 29, 2015 4:44 am Distinguish "want to relate to" (Alaska Slim) and "can relate to" (Bagheera).

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jul 29, 2015 4:59 am Honestly, I can't relate to Amuro, and don't particularly want to, either. And while I don't want to relate to Shinji, I'm forced to admit that I can , on several levels.

[]: Premise vs audience. B/             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed Jul 29, 2015 7:05 am Bagheera wrote: Honestly, I can't relate to Amuro, and don't particularly want to, either.

[]: Re: Premise vs audience. B/             Quote Post by Enki v.2 » Wed Jul 29, 2015 7:36 am Alaska Slim wrote: But Gundam already did that. Evangelion went a step further, to completely denied otaku an idol they would want to relate to, and instead, gave them someone who better embodied the qualities they shared, and realistically depicted the result.

[]: Re: Premise vs audience. B/             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed Jul 29, 2015 8:37 am Enki v.2 wrote: There are no positive characters in Evangelion. Everybody's screwed up. Even the dead. But, that doesn't mean that you can't identify with them! In fact, I think the reason why we identify so well with Eva characters is that they have extreme forms of pretty common pathologies (compare with Fate/Zero -- none of the audience is the sole heir to a long family line where children are taught magic by feeding them to parasites). I disagree here. Evangelion is about the premise moreso than it's about the viewer.

[]: Re: Premise vs audience. B/             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jul 29, 2015 10:00 am Alaska Slim wrote: While given that Amuro was the protagonist, there were other characters in the show one could relate to if they found him particularly wanting. The most popular character in the show afterall was Char, and on a more positive note, there was Bright Noa.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireball » Wed Jul 29, 2015 11:31 am I can relate to Char Enki pls, watch some Gundam first before making any more claims. I recommend the original Mobile Suit Gundam and War in the Pocket.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Wed Jul 29, 2015 1:49 pm Since this thread is about controversial anime opinions (and Gundam), here are my thoughts: Most Gundam shows are boring and completely interchangeable. They would be better off being a quarter of the length (12 eps instead of 50).

[]:             Quote Post by Tankred » Wed Jul 29, 2015 3:10 pm BrikHaus wrote: Most Gundam shows are boring and completely interchangeable. They would be better off being a quarter of the length (12 eps instead of 50).

[]: Re: Premise vs audience. B/             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed Jul 29, 2015 3:54 pm Bagheera wrote: I doubt much of anyone could relate to Char,

[]:             Quote Post by Enki v.2 » Wed Jul 29, 2015 7:25 pm Yeah, before I have a serious discussion about Gundam, I would need to actually watch it. But, I wasn't really talking about Gundam -- I was talking about Eva and how well it filled its niche in terms of deconstruction of mech tropes. I don't think Eva did a very good job of deconstructing otaku tropes, or the otaku personality in general; on the contrary, I think Otaku no Video did a better job of that without really trying, and Eva mostly directly addressed the fatalism of people steeped in a communal narrative dealing with reconstruction after a disaster. To the extent that Eva intentionally deconstructed anything in the real world and did so well, I think it deconstructed occupied Japan, not otaku. Certainly, otaku are not characterized by an inconsistent submission to authority and a pathological denial of self-worth (as Shinji is), or to a suicidal impulse expanded to the world (as Gendo is), or a sense that one should be content with one's place in life's machinery (as Rei is), or an obsession with revenge that cuts through an otherwise ordinary-seeming personality (as Misato is). The only person in Eva who is representative of otaku is the one character whose characterization is little more than a set of otaku sterotypes.

[]: Vehicle vs object. Bl             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed Jul 29, 2015 11:24 pm Enki v.2 wrote: Yeah, before I have a serious discussion about Gundam, I would need to actually watch it. But, I wasn't really talking about Gundam -- I was talking about Eva and how well it filled its niche in terms of deconstruction of mech tropes. I don't think Eva did a very good job of deconstructing otaku tropes,

[]:             Quote Post by Enki v.2 » Thu Jul 30, 2015 4:53 pm You can make the argument that otaku are characterized by escapism, but I don't buy it. Even if I did, the kind of escapism otaku engage in is extremely specific; other common forms of escapism do not qualify. Furthermore, otaku are not defined by an escape into fantasy but by an escape into feats of dedication -- memorization of the attributes of obscure foreign weapons, memorization of attributes of bad SF movies, building model kits and garage kits, collecting figurines. Kensuke is clearly an otaku character, but he's never shown engaging with fiction. Both Shinji and Rei are almost completely counter to the characterization of otaku, insomuch as they have no interests or personal passions; the only thing they share with otaku is a disconnection from society. However, every character we meet feels a disconnection from society, and many of them not only choose their own motivations over the good of the world (Misato) but actively betray humanity in order to further their own goals despite knowing that they are a lost cause (Ritsuko & Gendo); of all the characters, Shinji is the one who cares the most about humanity as a whole insomuch as he is unwilling to screw them over. His disconnection from society is half-hearted, like everything else about him; as a result, he cannot be considered in any way representative of otaku. On the other hand, he and every one of his classmates was born in the immediate aftermath of a world-wide disaster that the adult characters lived through in their youth. I think this is a lot more important than any commentary about otaku. While it's certainly not outside GAINAX's wheelhouse to comment on otaku culture, I don't see any indication of it within the text, and I feel like the commentary made afterward about it is no more decisive than Miyazaki's similar comments on the same subject, and perhaps even less so -- a group of people identifying as otaku made a show that was in many ways an ode to the shows they loved twenty years before, which was a financial failure during its initial run before being picked up and championed by an audience who recognized precisely zero of the references. ... I feel like I've derailed the thread by having a long discussion about eva in the only subforum on this site where we're not supposed to ;-)

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jul 30, 2015 6:28 pm Enki v.2 wrote: ... I feel like I've derailed the thread by having a long discussion about eva in the only subforum on this site where we're not supposed to ;-)

[]: The Evangelion dialectic. Bl             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Fri Jul 31, 2015 1:40 am Enki v.2 wrote: You can make the argument that otaku are characterized by escapism, but I don't buy it.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Mon Aug 03, 2015 8:56 pm Enki v.2 wrote: a group of people identifying as otaku made a show that was in many ways an ode to the shows they loved twenty years before, which was a financial failure during its initial run before being picked up and championed by an audience who recognized precisely zero of the references.

[]: Re: The Evangelion dialectic. Bl             Quote Post by GAP » Thu Aug 06, 2015 8:05 pm I guess it is merely Anno's way to reaching out to people who he identified with.
